evidence: Large callback-bag runtimes are used as primary dependency boundaries (e.g., `startupInitRuntime` + `StartupRuntime` in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`, `importerRuntime` in `goseg/importer/importer.go`, `workflowRuntime` in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`) with many function fields and grouped runtime tags.

Multiple packages expose mutable process-global dependency registries (`SetDefaultEventBroker` in `goseg/importer/importer.go` and `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`) while startup wiring mutates them (`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`), making effective dependencies implicit and boot-order sensitive.

Several runtime factory helpers are largely pass-through mapping layers without strong policy/translation (e.g., `runtimeContextOps`, `runtimeFileOps`, `defaultRuntime*` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_defaults.go`; function-pointer wrappers in `goseg/main.go`).

findings.summary: Core runtime boundaries rely on oversized callback bags, creating low-cohesion abstractions with weak compile-time contracts.
evidence: `startupInitRuntime`/`StartupRuntime` aggregate many unrelated callbacks (init, host, storage, background service concerns) behind one merged struct and validation by tag groups.

`importerRuntime` embeds three runtime structs and exposes many callback fields; behavior contracts are validated at runtime through `seams.NewCallbackRequirementsWithGroups(...)` instead of narrow compile-time interfaces.
`workflowRuntime` mixes event publication, transition policy, sleep/timing, DNS resolution, ship ops, config ops, and upload orchestration in one dependency object.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime bags into use-case-scoped interfaces/constructors (e.g., startup init, startup background, import upload, import provision, workflow transition emit). Replace string-tag group validation with typed constructor requirements so missing dependencies fail at compile time where possible.

findings.summary: Event broker abstraction is undermined by mutable package-global defaults, hiding dependency direction and creating order-dependent behavior.
evidence: `importer` and `shipworkflow` each hold a package-global default broker plus `SetDefaultEventBroker(...)` mutation points guarded by mutexes.
Startup wiring mutates these globals during initialization (`InitializeImporterFn` in startup runtime), so correctness depends on initialization sequence.
`events.NewGlobalDefaultBroker()` delegates through mutable process-default runtime lookups on each call, reinforcing implicit global indirection.
findings.suggestion: Make broker dependencies explicit in constructors and pass them through runtime instances end-to-end. Remove package-level setter mutation for production paths and reserve globals only for legacy compatibility wrappers.

findings.summary: Runtime factory layers add indirection but little behavioral value, increasing call depth and maintenance surface.
evidence: `runtime_defaults.go` has many helpers that primarily forward package functions into function fields (`runtimeFileOps`, `runtimeContextOps`, `runtimeNetdataOps`, `runtimeMinioOps`, `defaultRuntimeSnapshot`, etc.) with minimal policy.
`main.go` introduces wrapper methods (`bootstrapRuntime.bootstrap`, `.startServer`, `.runC2CCheck`) that mostly nil-check and forward to stored callbacks.
`runtimecontext/context.go` adds getter/setter wrappers over a global pointer state, and these are then re-wrapped again through orchestration runtime op structs.
findings.suggestion: Collapse pure pass-through wrappers where no policy/testing seam is needed; keep only seams that enforce behavior (timeouts, retries, fallback rules, translation). For remaining seams, centralize them in a smaller number of domain adapters instead of many per-function factories.
